idea.hook: How TIGHTLY packed are stars in a Globular Cluster? 🤯
facts: A typical globular cluster holds hundreds of thousands to over a million stars.
Despite this immense stellar population, it spans only 100-300 light-years across.
In their core, stars can be less than 0.1 light-years apart – about 40x closer than our Sun is to Proxima Centauri (~4.2 light-years).
idea.payoff: This ultra-dense 'star city' packs millions of stars into a region tiny compared to our galaxy's sparse neighborhoods!
idea.title: Globular Cluster: Star City Scale! ✨🌌

scenes.text: How TIGHTLY packed are stars in a Globular Cluster? 🤯
scenes.visual: Full screen, vibrant image of a massive globular cluster (e.g., M13, Omega Centauri), slowly rotating or subtly pulsing.
The globular cluster image. Numbers (e.g., '100,000', '500,000', '1,000,000+') appear and flash, indicating increasing star count, overlaying the cluster.
The globular cluster with a transparent sphere or circle overlaid, visually representing its 100-300 light-year diameter. The background subtly zooms out to show the cluster's relative size.

scenes.text:
Core stars <0.1 LY apart
(40x closer than Sun-Proxima!)
scenes.visual: Extreme zoom-in to the dense core of the globular cluster, showing individual stars almost touching. A visual indicator (e.g., two small dots and a line) measures the <0.1 LY distance, then transitions to showing a much larger distance for Sun-Proxima Centauri comparison.

scenes.text:
An ultra-dense 'star city'
in a galaxy of sparse neighborhoods!
scenes.visual: Transition from the dense core, zooming out to show the entire globular cluster. Then, zoom out further to show the cluster as a small, incredibly bright speck within a vast, dark, and mostly empty expanse representing a galaxy's arm or halo.
